one.zh: PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Objections to subpoena issued at the request of the wife directed to lawyers, an accountant and banks to produce documents – …
one.en: PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Objections to subpoena issued at the request of the wife directed to lawyers, an accountant and banks to produce documents – …

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Objections to subpoena issued at the request of the wife directed to lawyers, an accountant and banks to produce documents – Where the husband and the second to fourth respondents made blanket objection to the inspection of documents produced as to relevance, fishing and abuse of process – Where the husband withdrew a number of objections and the second to fourth respondents reduced their objections by reference to the schedules to the subpoena – Where orders are made to preserve claims of legal professional privilege – Where the objections are upheld in part – Where orders are made amending the schedules to the subpoena – No matters as to principle.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

These reasons determine objections to subpoena made by each of Mr Quinones (“the husband”), his parents Mr B Quinones (the second respondent) and Mrs B Quinones (the third respondent) (collectively referred to as “the husband’s parents”) and C Pty Ltd (“the fourth respondent”) issued at the request of Ms Quinones (“the wife”) for the production of documents directed to:
(a) National Australia Bank on 8 December 2025;

(b) Mr M of O Financial Services on 9 December 2025;

(c) Q Lawyers on 12 December 2025;

(d) P Bank on 12 December 2025.

The husband and the wife are engaged in substantive proceedings as to property settlement and spousal maintenance pursuant to Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The husband’s parents were joined as parties to the proceedings on 18 March 2025. The fourth respondent was joined as a party to the proceedings on 24 June 2025 in its capacity as the corporate trustee of the Quinones Family Trust (“the Trust”), being a discretionary trust controlled by the husband’s parents.
The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent filed a Reply on 30 October 2025. By way of Amended Points of Claim filed on 28 January 2026 they are plaintiffs seeking:
(a) Judgment for the husband’s parents for possession of the jointly held home of the husband and the wife at D Street Suburb E (“the [Suburb E] property”);

(b) Judgment for the husband’s parents in the sum of $1,837,000 or $1,097,000, together with interest, against the husband and the wife; and

(c) Judgment for the husband’s father and the fourth respondent in its capacity as trustee for the Quinones Family Trust against the husband and the wife in the sum of $754,532.84 plus interest.

In the alternative, the husband’s parents seek a declaration that the husband and the wife hold either 78 per cent or 46 per cent “of the value of [D Street]” beneficially for them by way of a resulting trust.
The husband submits to the primary and alternate relief sought by his parents and the fourth respondent. He did not articulate how or why he can concede to each.
The wife is currently conducting the litigation without legal representation. Her Points of Defence filed on 20 February 2026 opposes the relief sought by the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent. In the alternative, she seeks to set aside a document described later in these reasons as the 2016 loan agreement and any mortgage purporting to secure that loan facility upon the Suburb E property pursuant to s 106B of the Act.

BACKGROUND

The husband was born in 1979. He is 46 years old.
The wife was born in 1981. She is 44 years old.
The husband and the wife commenced cohabitation in 2010 and married in 2012. They have two children, X born in 2015 and currently aged 10 years, and Y born in 2016 and currently aged 9 years. They effected a separation on 16 September 2023. An Order for Divorce was made in 2024.
The husband and the wife are the shareholders of J Pty Ltd. J Pty Ltd was subcontracted by the fourth respondent to undertake building works during the marriage. The husband’s occupation is identified as a tradesperson.
During the period of the marriage the husband held a B class share in Quinones Pty Ltd. On 1 November 2023, just after separation, the husband’s share was transferred to his father. The wife seeks to set aside that transfer of shareholding pursuant to s 106B of the Act. The wife asserts that Quinones Pty Ltd traded in the development of real property during the marriage and had financial relationships with J Pty Ltd and the fourth respondent.
In March 2024, a final Apprehended Domestic Violence Order was made restricting and prohibiting the husband’s behaviour for a period of two years.
On 22 March 2024, the husband filed an Initiating Application in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) seeking orders as to the parenting of the children. His most recent relief is that as contained in his Further Amended Initiating Application filed on 25 November 2025 seeking orders for the parenting of the children and adjusting the property of the parties pursuant to s 79 of the Act. The wife filed a Response to an Initiating Application on 9 May 2024 seeking different orders as to the parenting of the children. Her most recent relief is that as contained in her Further Amended Response to an Initiating Application filed on 28 November 2025.
On 2 August 2024, interim orders were made on a defended basis regulating the parenting of the children broadly providing for the children to live with the mother, for the husband and the wife to have joint parental responsibility for the children and for the children to spend specified time with the husband.
On 27 June 2025, the proceedings were transferred to Division 1.
On 23 February 2026, the wife filed in Division 2 an Initiating Application seeking orders for spousal maintenance. On 13 March 2026, the wife’s Initiating Application was transferred to Division 1.

The integers of the disputes involving the non-marriage parties

The husband said in his affidavit filed on 23 January 2026:
In [early] 2010, my father (via his company, [C Pty Ltd]) purchased the [Suburb U] property, being vacant land at that time, for $950,000, with a view to developing it. In early 2010, I decided I wanted to build and live at the [Suburb U] property. My parents transferred the [Suburb U] property to me, and we entered into a Loan Agreement (2010 Loan Agreement) pursuant to which my parents loaned me the purchase price of $950,000 plus stamp duty of $39,850, totalling $989,850...
(Emphasis in the original)
The Amended Points of Claim of the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent plead:
Sometime before 3 March 2010, [the husband’s parents] and [C Pty Ltd] [the trustee of the family trust] entered into an agreement with [the husband].
The agreement pleaded at [6] included a term to the effect that [C Pty Ltd] would transfer its estate in fee simple in the property comprised in folio identifier [...], being the land situated at and known as [1 T Street], [Suburb U] NSW [...], to [Mr Quinones] in return for consideration in the sum of $950,000.
Particulars
Conversation between [the husband’s father], [Ms BB] and/or [the husband’s mother] and [the husband] occurring before 3 March 2010 during which [the husband’s father] said to [the husband] words to the effect that [C Pty Ltd] will transfer [T Street] to [the husband] on condition that if he sell it, then any profit or capital gain is to be repaid to [the husband’s parents] or [C Pty Ltd].
Transfer Form 01T dated 3 March 2010, having dealing number [...].
(Emphasis in the original)
The undated document referred to in the husband’s evidence as the 2010 loan agreement is between the husband’s parents and the husband. The fourth respondent, the legal owner of the property at 1 T Street Suburb U, is not a party to the document. The document is three paragraphs in length, recorded as “recitals”:
The Debtor acknowledges that he is indebted to the Creditors in the sum of Nine hundred and eight-nine thousand three hundred and fifty dollars ($989,350.00) (“Principal Sum”).
The Debtor covenants to repay the Principal Sum or such much thereof as may be owing from time to time to the Creditors within fourteen (14) days upon demand for such repayment being made.
The Debtor charges the property [1 T Street], [Suburb U] with repayment of the Principal Sum or so much thereof as may be owing from time to time.
(As per the original)
The wife says she had no knowledge of the 2010 loan agreement until it was disclosed to her during these proceedings. She puts into issue:
...The loan identifies [the husband’s parents] as Creditors and [the husband] as Debtor. At the time, [C Pty Ltd] aft the Trust was the registered owner of the property and was not named as a party to the loan notwithstanding [the husband’s parents] being directors of [C Pty Ltd]...
...
...To date, there has been no disclosure of this alleged loan for the April 2007 purchase and subsequent construction costs. Additionally, there is no mortgage registered on title between April 2007 to February 2015 identifying a loan.
[The husband’s parents] refer to the 2010 Loan in paragraph 7 (A) of their Points of Claim filed on 28 April 2025. They plead that a conversation between [the husband’s parents], [the husband] and [Ms BB] occurred prior to [the husband’s] 2010 Loan entrance “... during which [the husband’s father] said to [the husband] words to the effect that [C Pty Ltd] will transfer [T Street] to [the husband] on the condition that if he sells it, then any profit or capital gain is to be repaid to [the husband’s parents] or [C Pty Ltd]”. If the alleged conversation occurred prior to the 2010 loan execution I do not understand why terms addressing the repayment of capital gain were not included in the 2010 Loan, upon which emphasis is placed on the Principal amount only.

(Emphasis in the original)
The wife clearly puts into issue the integrity of the fact and terms of the advance, identifying inconsistencies between a loan transaction to facilitate the acquisition of real property and a joint venture type arrangement to share profit, and puts into issue the source of funds to acquire the legal interest in the property. The 2010 loan document does not record the agreement pleaded in paragraph 7A of the Amended Points of Claim filed by the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent.
The husband’s evidence thereafter is:
I borrowed $2,766,850 from my parents in total to assist with the purchase and the build of the [Suburb U] property. $989,850 of that sum was captured in the 2010 Loan Agreement. The further sum of $2,009,350 was captured in a further loan agreement with my parents and [C Pty Ltd], which I signed on 12 March 2015 (2015 Loan Agreement)... It was verbally agreed between my parents and I that my parents and their company, [C Pty Ltd], would retain all proceeds of sale when the property was later sold. This was where I had not made any financial contribution to the acquisition, development or maintenance of the [Suburb U] property. [The wife] and I moved into the [Suburb U] property of [sic] construction was complete at the beginning of 2012.
(Emphasis in original)
The husband concedes that he and the wife had separated at the time of his entry into the 2015 loan agreement.
The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent plead:
From about May 2010 to June 2012, [C Pty Ltd] and [the husband’s mother] advanced the following sums to [the husband] to finance building works at [T Street]:
Lender	Sum Advanced to [the husband]
[C Pty Ltd]	$578,166.17
[The husband’s mother]	$998,550.00
Total	$1,576,716.17
8A. [The husband] and [the wife] were not volunteers in relation to the advances made to them as pleaded above.
The monies referred to at [8] above were advanced as loans, repayable on the sale of [T Street].
Particulars
The terms are to be implied from the particulars at [7] above and from prior practice between the relevant parties, whereby [the husband’s parents] and/or [C Pty Ltd] would advance money to [Ms BB] and/or [the husband] to finance construction of their personal residences on condition that if those properties were sold, [the husband’s parents] and/or [C Pty Ltd] would be repaid from the sale proceeds for any funds advanced.
Bank statements for bank accounts held in the names of [C Pty Ltd], [the husband’s mother] and [the husband] for the period 3 March 2010 to 31 July 2012.
On or about [October 2015], [the husband] sold [T Street] for $3,575,000.

Particulars

Settlement sheet for sale of [Mr Quinones] to [Mr MM] in relation to [T Street].
The profit or capital gain earned by [the husband] from the sale of [T Street] was about $740,000 (Capital Gain).
(Emphasis in the original)
The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent do not plead the 2015 loan agreement identified by the husband or the verbal agreement asserted by the husband in paragraph 61 of his affidavit.
The pleaded calculation of profit or capital gain “earned” on the disposal of the Suburb U property is, on the material filed in the proceeding thus far, less than transparent. The wife identifies that the evidence of the husband as to the advance of $2,009,350 for the Suburb U property does not reconcile with the pleaded advances of his mother and the fourth respondent, or how his evidence as to “my parents and their company, [C Pty Ltd], would retain all proceeds of sale when the [Suburb U] property was later sold” sits with his earlier evidence and/or the case pleaded in the Amended Points of Claim.
I was not taken to the disclosure of any documents by the non-marriage respondents as to how the fourth respondent on behalf of the Trust recorded any “profit or capital gain” on the disposal by way of financial statements or taxation returns, or how the husband’s parents declared for revenue purposes any “profit or gain” produced by the disposal in their hands.
The husband’s evidence, after the disposal of the Suburb U property in October 2015 for $3,575,000 is:
On settlement of the [Suburb U] property, in accordance with the verbal agreement between my parents and me, my parents received the net proceeds of sale following payment of the usual costs of sale. However, my parents instructed the real estate agent to pay $438,574.43 (from bank settlement) and approximately $301,425.57 (the purchase deposit held by the agent) to me on settlement, by way of a further loan...
The husband then gives evidence:
[n mid-2016], we purchased [D Street], [Suburb E] ([Suburb E] property) for $2,350,000. I contributed about $740,000 towards the purchase price, being funds loaned to me by my parents on settlement of the [Suburb U] property. [The wife] and I also obtained a loan of $740,000 from the National Australia Bank and a further loan from my parents of $1,097,000. The amounts of $740,000 and $1,097,000 (totalling $1,837,000) were captured in a Deed of Loan dated 30 June 2016 (2016 Loan Agreement)...
(Emphasis in original)
The total value of the funds identified in paragraph 68 of the husband’s affidavit is 2,577,000. The value of stamp duty paid on the acquisition is uncertain.
The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent plead:
2016 Deed of Loan
On 30 June 2016, [the husband] and [the wife] mortgaged their estate and interest in [D Street] to [the husband’s parents] (Mortgage).
Particulars
Undated Mortgage Form 05M with annexure signed by [the husband and the wife].
Provisions contained in Memorandum [...].
Authority to complete and date granted by [the husband and the wife].
13. The Mortgage contained:
(a) a term to the effect that [the husband and the wife] covenanted with [the husband’s parents] that they will pay to [the husband’s parents] the “Principal Sum” of $1,837,000 and all other monies payable under the “Loan Agreement” (2016 Deed of Loan); and
Particulars

Annexure A to the Mortgage.
(b) a term to the effect that upon default, [the husband’s parents] are at liberty to exercise all or any of the powers of a mortgagee under the Real Property Act 1900 (NSW) or the Conveyancing Act 1919 (NSW).

Particulars

Clause 6 of Memorandum [...].
13A. Also on 30 June 2016, [the husband and the wife] made solemn declarations to the effect that they had:
(a) received independent legal advice regarding the Mortgage; and

(b) freely and voluntarily signed, among other things, the Mortgage and 2016 Deed of Loan.

Particulars

Declarations by Borrower signed by [the husband and the wife] dated 30 June 2016 and witnessed by [Mr JJ], solicitor.
13B. [The husband and the wife] were not volunteers in relation to the advances made in relation to the 2016 Deed of Loan.
On 4 July 2016, [the husband and the wife] became the registered proprietors, as joint tenants, of [D Street], having paid the purchase price of $2,350,000.
Particulars

Transfer Form 01T having dealing number [...].
Also on about 4 July 2016, [the husband’s parents] (as lenders) and [the husband and the wife] (as borrowers) entered into the 2016 Deed of Loan.

Particulars

Undated Deed of Loan between [Mr B Quinones] and [Mrs B Quinones] and [Mr Quinones] and [Ms Quinones].
16. The 2016 Deed of Loan contained:
(a) terms to the effect that [the husband’s parents] agreed to lend to [the husband and the wife], and [the husband and the wife] will repay to [the husband’s parents], the Principal Sum on the “Repayment Date”;
Particulars

Clauses 1.1 and 2.1 and the Dictionary of the 2016 Deed of Loan.
(b) a term to the effect that the Repayment Date was the earlier of:
(i) 28 days of a written demand by [the husband’s parents]; or

(ii) the sale of [D Street]; or

(iii) 20 years from the date of the 2016 Deed of Loan;

Particulars

Clause 2.1 and the Dictionary of the 2016 Deed of Loan.
(c) a term to the effect that [the husband and the wife] were liable to pay interest on the daily balance of the Principal Sum calculated from 4 July 2016 on monthly rests, payable on the Repayment Date:
(i) at the “Higher Rate” of 10 percent per annum, or

(ii) alternatively, at the “Lower Rate” of 8 percent per annum if [the husband and the wife] duly observed and performed all terms and conditions contained in or implied by the 2016 Deed of Loan;

Particulars

Clause 3 of the 2016 Deed of Loan.
(d) a term to the effect that [the husband and the wife] agreed that they had signed the Mortgage;

Particulars

Clause 4.1 of 2016 Deed of Loan.
(e) a term to the effect that [the husband and the wife] charged [D Street] with the performance by them of all terms and conditions of the 2016 Deed of Loan;

Particulars

Clause 4.2 of the 2016 Deed of Loan.
(f) a term to the effect that the Principal Sum will become immediately due and payable (at the option of [the husband’s parents]) upon the occurrence of an “Event of Default”; and

Particulars

Clause 6.1 of the 2016 Deed of Loan.
(g) a term to the effect that an Event of Default occurs if [the husband and the wife]:
(i) make a default in payment on the due date of any part of the monies secured or of any interest or other monies payable pursuant to the 2016 Deed of Loan;

(ii) commit a breach or makes default in the observance or performance of any agreement or obligation on their part pursuant to the 2016 Deed of Loan;

(iii) do or permit to be done any act or thing which may prejudice or adversely affect the validity or enforceability of the 2016 Deed of Loan; or

(iv) if any documentation or security furnished to [the husband’s parents] in relation to the 2016 Deed of Loan proves at any time to be imperfect in any respect.

Particulars

Clause 6.2 of the 2016 Deed of Loan.
The Principal Sum is the sum of the Capital Gain plus an advance by [the husband’s parents], via their directions to [C Pty Ltd] to do so, to [the husband and the wife] in the amount of $1,097,000 to fund part of the purchase of [D Street].

Particulars

Bank statement for account number [#27] for the period 1 July 2016 to 29 July 2016.
(Emphasis in the original)
The same firm of solicitors, Q Lawyers, advised each of the husband, the wife, the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent as to the 2016 loan agreement transactions. The wife contends that the husband diverted her from obtaining independent legal advice and that she did not receive independent advice as to the entry into the transactions now the subject of challenge.
It remains unclear as to the relevance of “the Capital Gain” identified in paragraph 17 of the Amended Points of Claim to the transaction recorded in the 2016 loan agreement. The agreement is broadly as to an advance of funds on specified terms and conditions absent apparent relationship to creating a beneficial interest in the property at D Street Suburb E (“the [D Street] property”).
As to the loan the husband and the wife obtained from the National Australia Bank to purchase the D Street property, the wife says:
Prior to 2016, I never entered a loan deed arrangement, had a bank loan or applied for a bank loan. Despite some of my attempts to engage and understand the process and the structuring of funds involved with these loans during the marriage, I was always excluded from such discussions and directed to sign for loans I had no understanding of. This exclusion has placed me at a disadvantage in these proceedings. During the course of which I was told I could obtain bank documents if my name was on them. I have since received various NAB loan documents that apart from signing, I was not given access to during the marriage. Upon retrieving the loan documentation form NAB, I've had to rely on my former solicitors to explain the processes involved with loan applications, upon which I have had to trace over ten (10) years of bank records trying to connect the pieces of our finances.
...
All the bank loan documents for the [Suburb E] Property were presented to me on or around the 1 July 2016, by [Mr NN] the families NAB business banker at the [OO Bank Centre] in Sydney at the direction of [the husband] and [the husband’s father], in finality, to sign without any explanation. It is only within these proceedings I gained an understanding of the alleged loaned funds for the [Suburb E] Property which include:
a. $740,000 NAB Loan account#46];
$1,607,354.07 funds from offset account [#34] (pursuant to purported funds loaned by [the husband’s parents] - Not aligned with 2016 Loan total); and
Total $2,347,354.07 required for settlement.
It is uncontroversial that both the husband and the wife received distributions of capital and income during the marriage from the Trust. The evidence refers to these as “paper distributions”. The wife says that the receipt of these distributions from the Trust are relevant to the quantum of the claim by the non-marriage parties.
The husband gives evidence that:
In 2018, [the wife] and I purchased a development at [EE Street], [Suburb E] ([EE Street] property) for $2,300,000, subject to a loan from National Australia Bank for $2,070,000, which was guaranteed by my father. My father loaned us $1,870,033.51 to build the [EE Street] property. The [EE Street] property was initially purchased in in the name of my company, [J Pty Ltd], where it was intended that the company would take that property on as a project however, [the wife] and I later decided to live there and so we purchased the property in joint names. The purchaser was amended prior to settlement. [The wife] was engaged in discussions with me and my parents regarding the purchase of this property every step of the way. We would not have been able to purchase the [EE Street] property without my father's assistance.
In June 2020, we sold the [EE Street] property for $5,400,000. After the usual costs of sale and repayment of the mortgage, I paid off the mortgage on the [Suburb E] property and repaid my father and [C Pty Ltd] $1,628,456, being part repayment of the build costs for the [Suburb E] property and for interest payments made by [the wife] and I for the bank mortgage each month, which my father loaned us as we otherwise could not have afforded to make those payments (which did not form part of the 2016 loan agreement). After that repayment there was a balance of $241,577.51 which remained owing for the build costs loaned by my father. Following repayment of those amounts, the balance of about $300,000 was used by [the wife] to purchase jewellery, and otherwise, applied towards living expenses.
The husband did not identify the terms of the agreement for the payment of “interest payments” or the value of those advances.
The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent plead:
Between about 29 May 2019 and 19 May 2021, [the husband’s father] and [C Pty Ltd] lent [the husband] and [the wife], at their express or implied request, the sum of $4,133,746.55 $1,870,033.51 (Additional Loan).
Particulars

The advances comprising the Additional Loan are set out in the table in Annexure A to these Points of Claim.
The Additional Loan contained, respectively, terms that the Additional Loan was:
(a) interest free during any period that the loan was not outstanding;

(b) to be repaid no later than upon any sale of Links Avenue; and

(c) may be repaid earlier than that time.

Particulars

Conversation of loan terms between [the husband’s parents, the husband and the wife.]
...
The Additional Loan consisted of the following types of transactions giving rise to a debt repayable by [the husband and the wife] to [the husband’s father] or [C Pty Ltd]: [the husband the wife] used the Additional Loan for the construction of a dwelling on Links Avenue.
(a) money lent by [the husband’s father] or [C Pty Ltd] to [the husband] or [the wife] for the construction of a dwelling on [EE Street];

(b) costs and expenses for the construction of a dwelling on [EE Street] paid by [C Pty Ltd] directly to the relevant supplier on [the husband’s] and [the wife’s] behalf at their express or implied request; and

(c) interest payments made by [the husband’s father] or [C Pty Ltd] directly to NAB in respect of the loan referred to at paragraph 19(a) above on [the husband’s] and [the wife’s] behalf at their express or implied request.

...

Demand from [the husband’s parents] and [C Pty Ltd]
From about 23 October 2019 15 March 2024 to 2 June 2024, [the husband and the wife] repaid [the husband’s father] and [C Pty Ltd] the sum of $3,379,213.71 $1,628,456 in respect of the Additional Loan (the Repayment).
Particulars

The Repayments are set out in the table in Annexure A to these Points of Claim.
The difference between the Additional Loan and the Repayment is $754,532.84 $241,577.51 (the Outstanding Debt).
On 24 September 2024, [the husband’s parents] and [C Pty Ltd] sent a letter of demand to [the husband and the wife] demanding payment of:
(a) the Principal Sum, plus interest owing pursuant to the 2016 Deed of Loan; plus
(b) $241,577.51 in respect of the Outstanding Debt.
Particulars

Letter of Demand from Swaab to Lander & Rogers and [Ms PP] dated 24 September 2024.
Despite demand, [the husband and the wife] have not paid any part of the:
(a) Principal Sum, plus interest owing pursuant to the 2016 Deed of Loan; or

(b) Outstanding Debt.

Annexure A to the Points of Claim identifies a series of payments from 29 May 2019 until 19 May 2021 including some made to J Pty Ltd, many without a reference to an invoice directed to the husband and/or the wife, and some of not insignificant value made by the husband’s father for no identified purpose to an unidentified recipient.
The wife in her Points of Defence denies the fact and terms of the additional loan from the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent for the EE Street property. She admits that some monies were paid to the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent from the sale of the property at EE Street Suburb E.

Orders as to the subpoena subject to objection

On 1 December 2025, orders were made granting the wife leave to issue subpoenas to the National Australia Bank, P Bank, O Financial Services and Q Lawyers.
The husband, the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent did not object to production of documents in compliance with the subpoenas. They each made blanket objection to inspection/copying of all the documents produced. Mr N and Mr M objected to the production of documents and the inspection of documents identified in each of their respective subpoenas.
On 13 March 2026, the following notations and orders were made:
THE COURT NOTES THAT:
Subpoenas were issued for the production of documents at the request of the wife to:
(a) National Australia Bank on 8 December 2025;

(b) [Mr M] of [O Financial Services] on 9 December 2025;

(c) [Q Lawyers] on 12 December 2025; and

(d) [P Bank] on 12 December 2025.

On 22 December 2025, [Mr N] of [Q Lawyers] filed a Notice of Objection to the subpoena directed to that firm that included a ground of legal professional privilege. It is uncontroversial that in these proceedings the wife is contended to have instructed that firm and received advice from that firm. None of the parties appearing today have instructed [Mr N] to take the objection to the subpoena.
[Mr M] of [O Financial Services] filed a Notice of Objection to the subpoena directed to him on 8 December 2025 including grounds as to an absence of apparent relevance, notwithstanding he is not a party to the proceedings, as to privilege, notwithstanding he is not a legal practitioner, as to privacy and as to an absence of particularity.
The second, third and fourth respondents filed Notices of Objection to the said subpoenas on 24 December 2025.
The husband filed a Notice of Objection to the said subpoenas on 15 January 2026.
On 12 March 2026 a senior judicial registrar made orders listing the Notices of Objection for hearing on 4 May 2026, recording amongst other things that no documents have been produced in respect of the subpoenas issued to:
(a) [Q Lawyers];

(b) [Mr M]; or

(c) National Australia Bank.
[P Bank] produced documents in compliance with the subpoena on 22 December 2025.
Subsequent to determining the outstanding objections to subpoenas, it is anticipated that a direction will be made for all parties to file an updated Undertaking as to Disclosure in compliance with ch 6 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).
For the purposes of the hearing of interim spouse maintenance on 23 March 2026, the wife relies upon her affidavit filed on 23 February 2026 and her Financial Statement filed on 23 February 2026.
...

THE COURT ORDERS THAT:

...

Notices of Objections
Orders made by a senior judicial registrar as to the Notices of Objection to subpoena by way of filing material and listing for hearing on 4 May 2026 are discharged.
Each of the said Notices of Objection are listed for hearing before me in person on 20 March 2026 at 2.15 pm.
On or before 16 March 2026 the solicitors for the husband are to serve [Mr N], and [Mr M] with a sealed copy of these orders.
In the event there is no appearance by or on behalf of an objector to the subpoenas on 20 March 2026, that objector ought to anticipate that their Notice of Objection will be dismissed.

(Emphasis in the original)
On 20 March 2026, the following notations and orders were made:
THE COURT NOTES THAT:
[Mr N] was placed on notice by way of an order made on 13 March 2026 that should he fail or neglect to appear at the listing today he ought to anticipate his Notice of Objection will be dismissed. [Mr N] did not appear at the listing today.
[Mr M] was placed on notice by way of an order made on 13 March 2026 that should he fail or neglect to appear at the listing today he ought to anticipate his Notice of Objection will be dismissed. [Mr M] did not appear at the listing today.

THE COURT ORDERS THAT:

Subpoena – [Mr N] c/o [Q Lawyers] issued on 12 December 2025
The Notice of Objection – Subpoena filed by [Mr N] c/o [Q Lawyers] on 22 December 2025 to the production of documents in response to a subpoena directed to [Mr N] c/o [Q Lawyers] issued at the request of the wife on 12 December 2025 is struck out.
The time for [Mr N] to comply with the production of documents as the recipient to the subpoena issued at the request of the wife be extended until 3 April 2026.
[Mr N] is placed on notice by way of these orders that in the event there is failure or neglect to comply with the terms of the subpoena, an order may be made directing him to attend court for the purposes of facilitating compliance with the subpoena.

Subpoena – [Mr M] c/o [O Financial Services] issued on 9 December 2025
The Notice of Objection – Subpoena filed by [Mr M] c/o [O Financial Services] on 8 January 2026 to the production of documents in response to a subpoena directed to [Mr M] c/o [O Financial Services] issued at the request of the wife on 9 December 2025 is struck out.
The time for [Mr M] to comply with the production of documents as the recipient to the subpoena issued at the request of the wife be extended until 3 April 2026.
[Mr M] is placed on notice by way of these orders that in the event there is failure or neglect to comply with the terms of the subpoena, an order may be made directing him to attend court for the purposes of facilitating compliance with the subpoena.

The Husband’s Notices of Objection – Subpoena filed on 15 January 2026
The Notice of Objection – Subpoena of the husband filed on 15 January 2026 to the production and inspection of documents produced by [Mr M] c/o [O Financial Services] in compliance with a subpoena issued at the request of the wife on 9 December 2025 is withdrawn.
The Notice of Objection – Subpoena of the husband filed on 15 January 2026 to the production and inspection of documents produced by [P Bank] in compliance with a subpoena issued at the request of the wife on 12 December 2025 is withdrawn.
The Notice of Objection – Subpoena of the husband filed on 15 January 2026 to the production and inspection of documents produced by the National Australia Bank in compliance with a subpoena issued at the request of the wife on 8 December 2025 is withdrawn.

...

(Emphasis in the original)

Variation to the terms of objection

Each of the husband, the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent withdrew their objection to the inspection of Q Lawyers’ 2016 loan agreement file. The solicitors acted for all parties to that transaction. They maintained a claim of legal professional privilege to the production of some categories of other documents sought to be inspected upon production by Q Lawyers. They flagged potential claims of legal professional privilege to some of the documents produced by Mr M (on behalf of O Financial Services).
The wife accepted that the husband, his parents, and the fourth respondent, ought to have the opportunity to have the first right to inspect the documents produced on subpoena by each of Q Lawyers and Mr M (on behalf of O Financial Services) to identify “confidential document[s]” within the meaning of ss 117 to 120 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) and thereby claim privilege from inspection. Orders will be made to ensure they have the first right of inspection and for the filing of evidence to establish the privilege claims, if made. Disputes as to whether privilege can be established, or as to any contentions of waiver of privilege, can be considered later.
During the hearing, the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent conceded that the scope of the categories of documents sought to be inspected is not confined to their claims alone but extends to forensic enquiries as between the husband and the wife in the broader s 79 dispute.
The husband’s parents and the fourth respondent reduced the scope of their objections by proposed amendments to the schedule to each subpoena as recorded in their submissions document filed on 20 March 2026. It is logistically problematic to record each amended schedule in these reasons. The husband joined in with the amended objections taken to by his parents to the subpoena to Q Lawyers. The terms of the proposed amendments to each schedule can be ascertained from the document filed on 20 March 2026.
The objections of the husband, the husband’s parents and the fourth respondent to the subpoena to Q Lawyers, to those identified amended schedules are:
(a) Not relevant;

(b) Serve no legitimate forensic purpose;

(c) A fishing expedition; and

(d) An abuse of process.

THE LAW

Provided subpoena have a legitimate forensic purpose, they will not be set aside. A legitimate forensic purpose has been held to include a sufficient apparent connection to justify the documents sought on production or inspection (see White & Tulloch & White [1995] FamCA 127; (1995) FLC 92-640; Hatton v Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia & Ors [2000] FamCA 892; (2000) FLC 93-038 at 87,606).

Relevance

The fundamental principle in relation to an objection relating to relevance is an absence of apparent relevance to the documents sought to be produced on subpoena (see National Employers’ Mutual General Insurance Association v Waind and Hill [1978] 1 NSWLR 372). Hence, a pivotal issue for determination is the apparent relevance of the documents sought to be produced to the proceedings. It is not a high threshold, the bar for establishing apparent relevance being set fairly low.
It has further been determined that providing it is “on the cards” that the documents sought would bear upon and have some relevance to the substantive proceedings, the subpoena process ought to be completed (Martin & Martin and Anor (No 2) [2014] FamCA 232 at [28], referring to Alister v R [1984] HCA 85; (1984) 154 CLR 404).
Whilst a document may not appear relevant in isolation, its relevance may only become clear in the context of other evidence. It may be seen as presumptuous for third parties to a marriage to assert what may or may not be relevant in the s 79 dispute between spouses under the Act, to which they are not privy (see Gilmore & Patrick [2022] FedCFamC1F 1015 at [37]).
These considerations lead to me being mindful, at this stage in the proceedings, not to summarily determine the apparent relevance of documents going to a substantive dispute between the parties.

Fishing/abuse of process

As identified by each party, as per Baumann and Ors & Rushbrooke and Anor [2016] FamCA 905 it is not legitimate to issue a subpoena:
(a) On the basis of an “outside chance” that something useful might turn up in the documents; and/or

(b) To fish with a view to determining whether the issuing party has a case at all such that they might reveal on return of the subpoena something capable of being probative.

A subpoena that does little more than “raise a speculative possibility that the document sought would assist” the resolution of a dispute is an abuse of process. Issuing a subpoena that is tantamount to discovery or disclosure of documents sourced from a party is an abuse of process.

CONSIDERATION

The wife submits that those parts of the schedule to the subpoenas subject to remaining objection “are directed to clearly identified issues” and are “neither speculative nor oppressive”. She says that the consistency and credibility of the asserted financial arrangements in issue are either supported by the surrounding financial conduct, historical records, and third-party dealings, or that these documents will establish an absence of integrity to the asserted case she is to meet.
The non-marriage parties submit that the documents sought in the categories remaining the subject of objection are plainly irrelevant and that the wife is fishing because “she does not know or understand why certain transactions happened”. The non-marriage parties assert that they have disclosed bank statements and other records that are “sufficient to substantiate the loans the subject of their Points of Claim” and that it is “impermissible for the [w]ife to use subpoena to conduct a wholesale review of 17 years of transactions for entities in which neither she nor the husband hold a financial interest”.
Each of the non-marriage parties have filed Undertakings as to Disclosure in compliance with ch 6 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) on 25 November 2025. They have disclosed to the wife all relevant documents and information in support of their claim in compliance with s 71B of the Act. The wife is not directing any subpoena to a party to produce documents. Implicitly, the documents sought by the wife to be produced on subpoena and inspected are not in the possession or control of the non-marriage parties. If they were, the non-marriage parties would clearly identify this to be the case. They have not done so. Hence, the documents sought to be inspected are not an alternate avenue to disclosure or discovery.
The non-marriage parties highlight an absence of evidence of connection between J Pty Ltd and the fourth respondent, as trustee for the Trust, engaging in real property developments.
I am satisfied, on a consideration of the matters recorded earlier in these reasons, that the wife has established from the pleadings and evidence the apparent relevance of a quantity of the material subject to amended objection. That material is directed to controversies in the litigation as to the fact, characterisation, treatment and enforceability of financial arrangements and circumstances as they existed between the husband, the husband’s parents, and the fourth respondent, together with other corporate entities, for the period prior to 2010 to 2015, and then as between those parties and the wife from 2016 to date. Quinones Pty Ltd is part of the matrix of these interrelated financial arrangements. The categories and date range of documents are identified with specificity. These categories of documents are not based on them having a speculative possibility of assisting the resolution of the factual disputes in the litigation. They engage with the secondary facts and inferences available to determine the disputed primary facts. The case constructed by the wife is understood by application of logical deduction, being the process of drawing inferences from facts established from contemporaneous documents and representations.
The marked objections to the schedules to the subpoenas as made by the non-marriage parties filed on 20 March 2026 will be amended to reflect this determination and are recorded in the orders at the commencement of these reasons.
The wife has not established that the remaining categories of documents sought subject to amended objection are currently apparently relevant or are “on the cards” as having probative forensic value to the determination of the issues in the proceeding as recorded earlier in these reasons. This includes categories of documents:
(a) As to Q Lawyers:
(i) Conveyancing files as to real properties in which the husband, the wife and J Pty Ltd did not have an identified interest in; and

(ii) “Alleged Matter No. [...]” and “Purported Matter No. [...]”, each category of file by reference to a number not being identified as relevant by the wife in submissions.

(b) As to Mr M, the accountant:

(i) Mr AA’s membership of the self-managed superannuation fund;

(ii) The benefits of the husband’s parents in the self-managed superannuation fund or instructions relating to those benefits;

(iii) The acquisition, construction and disposal of real properties at CC Street Suburb DD, 2 T Street Suburb U, FF Street Town GG and 1 V Street Suburb W; and

(iv) Shareholdings in and associated documents for HH Pty Ltd.

(c) As to National Australia Bank:

(d) Quinones Pty Ltd loans in relation to a real property;

(i) Email correspondence absent specification as to relevance from January 2014 to date;

(ii) The husband’s parents’ credit card accounts;

(iii) Account linkages; and

(iv) Documents as to all account “access”.

(e) As to P Bank:

(i) Linking schedules; and

(ii) Cross credit listings.

Hence the objections to these subpoenas will be allowed in part. The balance of the marked objections in the schedules of the non-marriage parties will be dismissed.
The parties did not have the opportunity to make submissions as to costs sought of or incidental to the determination of the Notices of Objection. Costs will be reserved.
For all the above reasons, I make the orders as set out herein.
